data.title: WTT Finals: Truls Moregard aims to create more happy memories of Hong Kong
data.description:
Swedish table tennis star Truls Moregard has targeted another Hong Kong career boost as he returns to the city still savouring the memories he made last time.
Olympic silver medallist Moregard is in the line-up for next month’s year-ending World Table Tennis (WTT) Finals, and is hoping he finds Hong Kong, which will host it, a happy hunting ground again.
As the world No 5 turned his attention to proceedings at Hong Kong Coliseum from December 10 to 14, he recalled the step up he took during an ITTF World Tour leg at Queen Elizabeth Stadium in 2019.
“I have many good memories,” the 23-year-old said. “I was very young and not in the top 100 in the world. I was trying to make a statement, and after that tournament, I went on to be top-100, so it was like a little barrier I took there in Hong Kong.
“I also got many days to visit the city, and I rode on the ferry many times and climbed up to The Peak once. I was having a great time … I love the city, it’s very cool.”

Moregard added that he was “very excited to eat some good food”, but his first priority is having another crack at China’s finest.
“They are still the best players, and they have the biggest span of players, and in team events they will always be almost impossible to beat,” he said. “But I feel that in many tournaments, we are getting closer. On a good day, we can win some big titles.
“We can challenge them, and I think it’s fun for us, and them. [For a few years] everything felt impossible, and now we [have] actually quite a lot of players that can challenge them, so I’m very happy to be part of that section.”
He proved that by beating top seed Wang Chuqin in the second round at last year’s Olympics in Paris and going on to take silver, with Wang’s China teammate Fan Zhendong claiming gold. Moregard also won men’s team silver with Sweden, losing the final to China.
“China is great for our sport,” he said. “What they are doing, they are bringing so many people to watch in every tournament. It’s not fun for them if there’s no challenge. We want to challenge them to see how good we can become.
“As a kid, I always heard it’s impossible to win the big titles because of all the fantastic Chinese players, so that’s what I’m striving for.”
Since becoming the first from his country since the legendary Jan-Ove Waldner in 2000 to win an Olympic medal, Moregard has added August’s Europe Smash and this month’s WTT Champions Montpellier titles to double his career crowns.
“Many people thought and spoke about [Paris] being a one-time thing,” he said. “But I wanted to show that I can have this level and maintain this level all year long.”

data.title: Indonesia floods and landslides kill 17 on Sumatra island
data.description:
Rescuers recovered more bodies in the search for dozens of people buried under landslides or swept away after torrential rains unleashed flash floods and triggered landslides on Indonesia’s Sumatra island, increasing the death toll to 17 and leaving six people missing, officials said on Wednesday.
Rescue teams were struggling to reach affected areas in six regencies of North Sumatra province after the monsoon rains over the past week caused rivers to burst their banks, tearing through hilly villages as mud, rocks and trees tumbled down, leaving destruction in their wake, the National Police said in a statement.
Rescuers by Wednesday had recovered at least five bodies and three injured people in the worst-hit city of Sibolga and were searching for four villagers who were reported missing, the statement said.
In the neighbouring district of Central Tapanuli, landslides hit several homes, killing at least a family of four, and floods submerged nearly 2,000 houses and buildings.
Rescuers retrieved seven more bodies in South Tapanuli district, raising the death toll to eight, after floods and landslides also uprooted trees, prompting more than 2,800 residents to flee to temporary shelters and injuring 58 others, said National Disaster Mitigation Agency spokesperson Abdul Muhari.

He said landslides also hit 50 houses in North Tapanuli district and destroyed at least two main bridges in the region. Floods had cut off a bridge in Mandailing Natal and submerged hundreds of houses in the hilly district and its neighbouring Padang Sidempuan city, while a main road was blocked by mud and debris on Nias island.
Videos on social media show water cascading down rooftops as panicked residents scramble for safety. In some areas, flash floods rose rapidly, transforming streets into raging torrents carrying tree trunks and debris.
Sibolga police chief Eddy Inganta said emergency shelters have been set up and authorities urged residents in high-risk zones to evacuate immediately, warning that continued rainfall could trigger more landslides after six landslides in the hilly city flattened 17 houses and a cafe.
“Bad weather and mudslides hampered the rescue operation,” Inganta said, adding that access remains limited as rescuers battle harsh conditions.

Tuesday’s disasters occurred the same day the National Disaster Mitigation Agency declared the official end of relief efforts in two areas of Indonesia’s main island of Java after 10 days of operations.
More than 1,000 rescuers had been deployed to search for people buried under landslides triggered by torrential rains that left 38 people dead in Central Java’s districts of Cilacap and Banjarnegara.
At least two people in Cilacap and 11 in Banjarnegara were still unaccounted for when the operations ended, as unstable ground, bad weather and the depth and extent of the landfill material posed a high safety risk to rescue teams and residents, the agency said.
Heavy seasonal rain from about October to March frequently causes flooding and landslides in Indonesia, an archipelago of 17,000 islands where millions of people live in mountainous areas or near fertile floodplains.

data.title: AI start-up offers local alternative to Google’s TPU as China seeks to cut Nvidia reliance
data.description:
Chinese AI chip start-up Zhonghao Xinying has emerged as a home-grown alternative to Nvidia with a new tensor processing unit (TPU), just as Google shakes up Nvidia’s lock on the market by selling its in-house tensor chips directly to major tech firms.
The Hangzhou-based firm, also known as CL Tech, said its self-developed general-purpose tensor processing unit (GPTPU) went into mass production as early as 2023. Its flagship chip, dubbed Chana, delivers up to “1.5 times the compute performance” of Nvidia’s A100 tensor core graphics processing unit (GPU), while “cutting energy consumption by 30 per cent for equivalent large-model workloads and reducing per-unit compute cost to 42 per cent of Nvidia’s”, according to the company.
GPUs are flexible, general-purpose parallel processors originally built for graphics applications but now widely used for AI training and inference. TPUs, a type of application-specific integrated circuit, developed by Google for neural-network training and inference, offer higher efficiency and throughput for certain deep learning workloads.
Nvidia’s GPUs are considered the backbone of the global AI boom, making the firm the world’s most valuable company, yet many customers are keen to reduce their dependence on the US chip giant.

Google’s recent decision to supply TPUs directly to Anthropic and Meta Platforms, instead of only providing access through its cloud services, has positioned it more as a direct rival to Nvidia. The move even rattled market confidence in Nvidia’s long-term grip on the sector.
Chinese AI developers began to seek alternatives to Nvidia after Washington restricted access to the US firm’s most advanced products.
Zhonghao Xinying was founded in 2018 by Yanggong Yifan, a Stanford and University of Michigan-trained electrical engineer who previously worked on chip architectures at Google and Oracle. He was involved in the full design-to-deployment cycle of Google’s TPU v2, v3 and v4, according to the Chinese company.
CTO and co-founder Zheng Hanxun, a graduate of the University of Southern California, previously worked in chip-design roles at Oracle and Samsung Electronics’ research and development centre in Austin, Texas, according to his LinkedIn profile.
In an interview published in September by Sincere Capital, an investor in the start-up, Yanggong said that during his years in Silicon Valley “it became clear that a US-China tech war was inevitable”, adding that AI and the compute infrastructure behind it would “become the core battleground”.

Yanggong said Zhonghao Xinying’s TPU features “fully self-controlled IP cores, a custom instruction set and a wholly in-house compute platform”. “Our chips rely on no foreign technology licences, ensuring security and long-term sustainability from the architectural level,” he was quoted as saying.
“We have achieved a 1.5x performance increase while reducing power consumption to 75 per cent using a manufacturing process that is an order of magnitude lower than that of leading overseas GPU chips,” Yanggong said in a June speech.
As a fabless chip company, Zhonghao Xinying outsources the fabrication of its chips to foundries, but it has not publicly revealed its manufacturing partners.
The company also introduced Taize, a large-scale compute cluster linking 1,024 Chana units, capable of supporting training for trillion-parameter-class foundation models. Yanggong told an industry conference in June that a “next-generation TPU” was in the works, without giving a timeline.
In August, Zhonghao Xinying announced plans to acquire Shanghai-listed auto-parts maker Tip Corporation, a move that pushed the latter’s shares from roughly 30 yuan at the time to 140 yuan today.
Financial filings for the acquisition revealed that in 2023 Zhonghao Xinying generated 485 million yuan (US$68.4 million) in revenue and 81.3 million yuan in net profit, largely from the Chana TPU. Revenue rose to 598 million yuan in 2024, with net profit edging up to 85.9 million yuan, but for the first half of this year it reported revenue of just 102 million yuan and a loss of 144 million yuan.
Zhonghao Xinying signed a performance-guarantee agreement with its investors that requires the company to go public by the end of 2026, or a share buy-back clause will be triggered. Tip Corporation said in a recent filing that the chip start-up had already begun work on a separate initial public offering.

data.title: China’s mega dredger joins ranks of the world’s largest after passing sea trials
data.description:
China has boosted its strategic capacity from land reclamation to deep-sea mining and pipelines, with the successful completion of sea trials for Asia’s largest trailing suction hopper dredger (TSHD).
The Tongjun – China’s first domestically designed super dredger – completed its five days of testing in the Yellow Sea off the northeastern Chinese coast on Tuesday, three months after its launch, according to state news agency Xinhua.
With a hopper capacity of more than 38,000 cubic metres (1.3 million cubic feet) and twin 9,000 kilowatt mud pumps, the Tongjun and its sister ship Junguang were launched in August, from shipyards in the northern city of Tianjin and Guangzhou in the country’s south, respectively.
Developer China Communications Construction Company (CCCC) has not announced a testing plan for Junguang, which has the same capacity and similar design as the Tongjun at 198 metres (650 feet) long, with a beam of 38.5 metres and a depth of 18 metres.

Both dredgers were designed to extract materials from a maximum depth of 120 metres, with their improved suction making it possible to fill the large hoppers within 90 minutes, Xinhua reported.
The sea trials included testing of the Tongjun’s twin mud pumps, capable of sending dredged materials over 12km (7.5 miles), and its huge capacity – it can raise the level of a standard football field by 5 metres with a single dredging operation, according to the report.
The capacity of the Chinese mega dredgers ranks behind only the Cristobal Colon, built by Spanish shipbuilder La Naval in 2009 for the Belgian firm Jan De Nul, and its sister ship the Leiv Eiriksson, which both have a hopper capacity of 46,000 cubic metres, making them the largest in the world.
The successful test shows that China can domestically produce the two kinds of dredging equipment – the TSHD which is best suited for soft materials like sand, silt and mud, and the large cutter suction dredger (CSD), which can also handle hard soil and rock.
CCCC launched the Chinese-designed CSD Tian Kun in 2017, with a capacity of 6,000 cubic metres per hour and capable of sending dredged material over 15km, digging up to 35 metres beneath the sea floor.

Both kinds of dredgers can pump dredged material to a site in a method called “rainbowing” and are frequently used in seaport and waterway dredging, as well as land reclamation and a range of deep-sea activities.
CSD and TSHD dredging equipment is indispensable in deep-sea sand extraction, mining, trench excavation and backfilling, along with gravel bed construction and the laying of underwater pipelines.
China has invested heavily in its dredging industry, building about 200 vessels since 2006 and becoming one of the world’s biggest equipment manufacturers.
Before the Tian Kun there was the Tian Jing, or “Celestial Whale”, commissioned in 2010 and capable of excavating 4,500 cubic metres (159,000 cubic feet) of sand per hour and pumping it up to 6km (3.7 miles) away.
The Tian Jing is best known for its contribution to the “Great Wall of Sand” in the South China Sea, reportedly spending 193 days between September 2013 and June 2014 moving among five reefs in the disputed Spratly Islands.
The dredging team broke up the underwater corals, pumping and stacking the material onto submerged atolls – Fiery Cross, Mischief and Subi – to construct the largest land features in the South China Sea, with airports, radar arrays and missile positions.
After the “Great Wall of Sand” was completed, the Tian Jing project won the highest prize for 2019 in China’s National Science and Technology Progress Awards.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: Indonesia’s Gaza peacekeeping pledge raises risk of clash with Hamas
data.description:
Indonesia’s pledge to send troops to Gaza as part of a US-backed international stabilisation force (ISF) has prompted warnings by analysts that the Muslim-majority nation could be drawn into combat operations or accused of abandoning the Palestinian cause.
They said Jakarta’s long-standing support for Palestinian statehood was in contrast with the ISF’s mandate – particularly if the latter involves disarming Hamas – and the move could spur claims that Indonesia is siding with the US and Israel.
Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto has repeatedly promised to send up to 20,000 troops to Gaza to help with peacekeeping operations.
Details of the planned deployment became clearer in the past few days following an endorsement of a US-drafted resolution for a peace plan in Gaza by the UN Security Council on November 17. The resolution was backed by 13 countries, with two abstentions from China and Russia.
On Monday, Agus Subiyanto, commander-in-chief of the Indonesian National Defence Forces (TNI) said the military was in the process of selecting personnel for the peacekeeping troops for Gaza.
“The selection stage for the [Indonesian] peacekeeping contingent is now under way. The plan is for it to be led by a three-star general,” Agus told reporters on Monday.
The general would lead three battalions, which would focus on health, construction engineering and aid and mechanical support, Agus said. The troops would be equipped with helicopters, C-130 Hercules transport aircraft and two Navy hospital ships, he said.

“When there is clarity [about the mission], we will send recce [reconnaissance] personnel there, as a precursor, to ascertain the situation and the placement of troops.”
Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin previously said that the Indonesian military was waiting for a “political decision from the government”, as well as approval from Israel and Arab states such as Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Egypt, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates before officially joining the ISF.
Risks abound
Apart from the establishment of the ISF, whose mandate will last until 2027, the resolution also approved the creation of the Board of Peace, an international transitional body that would set up and fund a committee comprising neutral Palestinian technocrats to temporarily govern Gaza.
The ISF will work with the board alongside vetted and trained Palestinian police personnel tasked with – among others – securing border areas and permanently disarming militias, including Hamas. This proposal has sparked alarm in Indonesia over a potential entanglement of its peacekeeping troops in the conflict.
“If Indonesia later sends peacekeeping forces to Gaza, do not let the TNI be tasked with disarming Hamas. We must prevent a scenario where the TNI could clash physically with Hamas in Gaza, because this would tarnish the TNI’s image at home and abroad,” former Indonesian ambassador to the US, Dino Patti Djalal, said in a social media post on November 21.
Sudarnoto Abdul Hakim, head of foreign relations and international cooperation at the Indonesian Ulema Council, said the UN resolution was “unfair” to the Palestinians as it would not guarantee either a free Palestine or Israel permanently leaving the Gaza Strip.
“It is very likely that we will also face Hamas in Gaza. Indonesia has always fully defended Palestine and Gaza, but now it could actually face off against friends in Palestine. This must be avoided,” Sudarnoto said.

Shofwan Al Banna Choiruzzad, an associate professor of international relations at the University of Indonesia, said that Indonesia should get approval from the people in Gaza before deploying its troops.
“The question is, will the ISF be able to control Israel, or will it be controlled by Israel? The risks are significant. Indonesia needs to carefully consider whether its good intentions for contributing to peace do not lead to Indonesia becoming an instrument of colonialism,” Shofwan said.
Hamas and other non-governmental factions in Gaza have rejected the UN Security Council’s resolution.
“Assigning the international force with tasks and roles inside the Gaza Strip, including disarming the resistance, strips it of its neutrality and turns it into a party to the conflict in favour of the occupation,” Hamas said in a statement published online on November 17.
Geopolitical benefits
In Gaza, Indonesian troops are likely to focus on protecting the distribution of humanitarian aid and the reconstruction of the war-torn enclave, according to Ahmad Umar, an international politics fellow at Aberystwyth University.
“The troops we will send are reconstruction and relief battalions. We need to see whether Uzbekistan is doing the same thing with its peacekeeping force, or whether it is doing something different,” Ahmad said. Uzbekistan has also been mentioned as another potential contributor to the ISF.
Ahmad added it was important for Indonesia to get approval from Arab countries “so that when we send troops, our diplomatic position is stronger. If we don’t seek approval from Arab countries, we’re somewhat vulnerable”.


Muhadi Sugiono, a lecturer in international relations at Gadjah Mada University, said while joining the ISF was risky, it could bring benefits for Jakarta, including a closer bilateral relationship with Washington.
“Trump is deeply involved in the Gaza peace plan personally. When Indonesia follows Trump’s approach, we automatically become friends with the US. Bilaterally, we may benefit, perhaps in our tariff deal with the US,” Muhadi said.
Ahmad added that being part of the ISF would not affect Indonesia’s ties with Beijing and Moscow, as they did not reject the UN Security Council’s resolution, which paved the way for the establishment of the force.
“This will not [hurt] our diplomatic relations with China and Russia, but we need to communicate with them informally, because these two countries are quite influential and have other interests [in the conflict in Gaza],” he said.

data.title: Your Hong Kong weekend drinks guide for November 28-30 – plus December 1
data.description:
As the year comes to an end, bars across Hong Kong are throwing holiday festivities – while one of the city’s hottest venues bids us farewell.
Start the weekend at Harbour City as farm-to-glass concept Glass Gardener takes over Hexa, or “wine” down this Saturday as 67 Pall Mall – the private members’ club for wine lovers – celebrates 10 years in Hong Kong with a selection of 2015 vintages at 67 on Hollywood.
Be sure to make time for Hong Kong’s own bastion of rum, The Daily Tot, as it hosts farewell parties on November 28 and November 29 before sadly closing – for now? – on December 2.
The nightlife community also promises to keep the weekend going for a good cause through Monday as eight of Hong Kong’s best mixologists rally to support one of their own.
Friday, November 28
Hexa x Glass Gardener

What could be better than sweeping night views of Hong Kong with cocktails made by Hong Kong’s premier farm-to-glass nomadic concept? Glass Gardener, co-founded by Tony Hsu and Henry Ho, is taking over the bar at contemporary Cantonese restaurant Hexa at Harbour City. Hsu and Ho are collaborating with Rooster Rojo and Paragon Cordial to serve drinks inspired by Chinese tea.
Where: Hexa, Shop OTE 101, G/F, Ocean Terminal, Harbour City, Canton Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
When: 6pm-10pm
Saturday, November 29
The Daily Tot’s A Final Farewell

Hong Kong bar-goers were shocked to hear that Singular Concepts would be closing its Cuban-inspired rum hotspot, The Daily Tot, on December 2. According to a press release, the concept is closing due to “economic realities of the post-pandemic landscape, including the rising costs of operations”. However, this is not a true final farewell as Singular Concepts CEO David Sit says in the same release, “We hold onto the concept and the spirit of The Daily Tot and hope to be back in the near future.” Watch this space …
The team has planned A Final Farewell for November 28 and November 29. On the latter date, DJ Minou, curator of the Black Out Brunches from the bar’s Covid-19 era, hosts the final edition of Rum & Riddim.
Where: Shop E, G/F, Felicity Building, 58 Hollywood Road, Central
When: 8pm till late
Sunday, November 30
67 Pall Mall: A Toast to Ten Years

There’s arguably no better way to “wine” down your weekend than by spending a night at 67 on Hollywood, where members’ club 67 Pall Mall is raising A Toast to Ten Years with a series of 67 wines with a 2015 vintage, representing the first year the club was in Hong Kong.
Highlights range from the Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clavoillon from Domaine Leflaive, a balanced chardonnay from Burgundy; to the Chapter & Verse Tempranillo, a creamy, almost minty, medium-bodied wine from Huailai County in Hebei, China. The selection also includes a Josmeyer Les Pierrets Riesling for those looking for more texture and variety.
Where: 67 on Hollywood, 41 Hollywood Road, Central
When: 4pm till late
Monday, December 1
Hospitality Titans Unite – Fundraiser for Luca

Eight of Hong Kong’s most celebrated mixologists are coming together to take over Barcode to raise funds for Luca Andrei (Lockdown, Bianco & Rosso, Pernod Ricard and more). In the week surrounding the World’s 50 Best Bars festivities, according to Midnight Espresso Communications, Andrei sustained multiple complex fractures to his right leg, which require intensive surgeries and an 18-month recovery period.
To support him, 100 per cent of the proceeds from this event are going towards his medical and living expenses. Proof & Co and Ratafia Rossi are sponsoring the proceedings with heavy support from Midnight Espresso Communications, with all-star mixologists in attendance: Lorenzo Antinori (Bar Leone, Montana), Benjamin Boyce (Industry Icon, previously Club 1880, La Petite Maison), Aled Burt (Bourke’s), Beckaly Franks (Artifact, Call Me Al), Axel Gonzalez (Terrible Baby), Nic Ho (Aer, Dead Poets), Amir Javaid (Socio, Gossip) and Kallum Wu (Proof & Co).
Extend your weekend to support a good cause and community member – and enjoy a world-class drink in the process.
Where: Barcode, UG/F, 45 Cochrane Street, Central
When: 7pm onwards

data.title: Chinese tourism to Russia soars as travellers pivot from under-fire Japan
data.description:
As China’s tourists scrap plans to visit Japan en masse amid an ongoing diplomatic feud, many appear to be pivoting to an alternative destination: Russia.
Chinese travel firms have reported a surge in Russia-related flight and hotel bookings in recent weeks compared with the same period last year, with analysts saying the country’s winter scenery made it a natural replacement for Japanese hotspots like the northern island of Hokkaido.
Russia hotel bookings made by Chinese travellers in the two weeks ending on Monday for stays in December were up more than 50 per cent year on year, according to travel marketing and technology firm China Trading Desk.
Some travellers who had booked trips to Hokkaido – famous in China for its ski resorts and hot springs – were switching to Russia due to its similar climate, said Subramania Bhatt, the company’s CEO.
“For travellers who were originally considering Hokkaido for snow, winter landscapes and outdoor activities, it’s relatively easy to substitute with Russia’s winter products – in Moscow or St Petersburg plus the Far East or Arctic regions – because the climate and activity profile are quite similar,” Bhatt said.
Fliggy, a major Chinese travel platform, said flight bookings to Russia over the past two months had nearly doubled compared with the same period last year. Fliggy is owned by Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
“Russia offers a unique combination of natural scenery, cultural charm and affordability, particularly its distinctive winter travel experiences such as aurora hunting and admiring the blue ice of Lake Baikal,” the company told the Post in a statement.
“The current snow season and the anticipated convenience of visa policies are also positive factors further encouraging Chinese tourists to travel to Russia.”
Tensions between Beijing and Tokyo escalated this month after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested her country could deploy its military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait.


Beijing issued a travel warning on November 14 urging Chinese citizens to avoid visiting Japan, citing safety concerns. Hundreds of thousands of Chinese tourists have since cancelled their air ticket bookings to the country, and domestic airlines had cancelled flights on 12 Japan routes as of Monday.
Meanwhile, the number of passengers flying from China to the eastern Russian city of Vladivostok between October 1 and November 25 reached 67,000, a 37 per cent increase compared with the whole of October and November last year, Vladivostok International Airport data showed. The city’s weekly flight connections to China have also risen from 35 to 43.
“Vladivostok is interesting by itself as a city with European ambience, Christian culture, military history and with China-friendly infrastructure,” the airport said in a statement.
Russian President Vladimir Putin’s recent announcement that his country would lift visa requirements for Chinese travellers “in the very near future” – a response to China granting Russians 30-day visa-free access in September – also appears to have helped put Russia on the map as a travel destination.
The volume of Chinese users searching for and viewing Russia-related tourism products in the 48 hours after November 18 – the day Moscow indicated visa-free entries were on the way – tripled compared with the same period during the previous week, China Trading Desk calculated.
“Once you add visa-free entry and the perception that Russia is less politically sensitive than Japan right now, that substitution becomes even easier for agencies to suggest and for travellers to accept,” Bhatt said.
Russia is not the only country benefiting from the drop in Chinese tourism to Japan. Some Chinese travellers are also rebooking for trips to South Korea and countries in Southeast Asia, according to Bhatt.
In Russia, the most popular destinations for Chinese tourists include Moscow, St Petersburg, Murmansk and Minsk, as well as Vladivostok, Fliggy said.
It added that Chinese travellers were “increasingly drawn” to Murmansk for the chance to see the aurora borealis – also known as the northern lights – without having to pay the high prices often seen in Nordic countries.
China-Russia relations remain warm as the two sides step up trade, with Russia often exporting resources that China needs for its factories. Russia has leaned on China for economic support in recent years, especially after Western countries placed sanctions on Moscow over the war in Ukraine.

data.title: Japan man defies family expectations, rejects traditional career path, wears women’s clothing
data.description:
A man in Japan has defied his family’s expectations by rejecting a civil service examination, wearing women’s clothing and living in a flat filled with rubbish.
His 2022 story has recently gained attention in China amid rising reports of extreme parenting.
Mizuki, 26, is a graduate of the prestigious University of Tokyo and was raised in a strict, success-driven household, according to Fuji TV.
His father, a scholar, kept his distance, while his mother pushed him relentlessly to excel academically, treating his achievements as a return on her investment.
After his parents divorced, Mizuki lived with his mother, who frequently scolded him.

Obedient and driven, he initially followed a traditional path: excelling at school, attending top universities and striving for a stable career.
But despite his outward success, Mizuki said he felt empty.
Struggling with his identity and discomforted by the changes in his appearance during puberty, he began wearing women’s clothing.
He saw this as a way to break free from his mother’s control and to stop being the “perfect son”.
After graduating, Mizuki worked as a sales associate at a women’s clothing shop, quickly understanding customer preferences and enjoying the role.
But he grew bored of the routine and quit.

He then switched to the information technology (IT) industry, but the office environment only reminded him of his mother’s controlling influence.
Realising his life had been shaped by others’ expectations, Mizuki refused when his mother insisted he take a civil service examination.
She kicked him out and said: “Why should I continue to support you?”
In Japan, civil service jobs are seen as stable, well-paying and offer long-term career growth.

Mizuki left home, cutting off all contact with his mother.
He rented a small flat in Tokyo for about 30,000 yen (US$190) a month, gradually filling it with discarded items like delivery boxes, old clothes and broken appliances.
While these may seem like rubbish to others, he saw them as a source of companionship.
Now, Mizuki makes a living by live-streaming in women’s clothing, chatting with fans who are drawn to his gentle voice.
“I am who I am, and I hope to present myself confidently,” he said.

Mizuki’s story has sparked heated debate on mainland social media.
One person said: “I respect him for choosing his own path. His mother never truly cared for his emotional needs.”
Another said: “His story reminds me of many children in China. Under immense pressure to become elite, they grow up stressed and yearning to escape. Some graduates from prestigious schools even choose unconventional jobs, like becoming security guards or selling food at street stalls.”
However, others disagreed.
One online observer said: “Mizuki has wasted his mother’s support and squandered the education he received. He should clean up his life, starting with his cluttered home, and maybe consider becoming a teacher to help others who are lost.”

data.title: Meet Landman star Sam Elliott’s only daughter, musician Cleo Rose Elliott – but what happened with her mother, Katharine Ross?
data.description:
The second season of Taylor Sheridan’s neo-Western saga about the cutthroat oil industry in West Texas, Landman, is off to a phenomenal start. According to streaming data site FlixPatrol, it is the third most-watched TV series on Paramount+ at the time of writing, with Sheridan’s very own Tulsa King taking the top spot.


Though Landman’s second season boasts powerful performances by actors like Billy Bob Thornton, Demi Moore, Ali Larter and Jacob Lofland, it is 81-year-old Sam Elliott who is stealing the show. The 1883 star plays protagonist Tommy’s father, TL Norris, in this season.
In an interview with TV Line, the celebrated actor described his character as someone who is “living his life just full of regret”. He has a complicated relationship with his son, Tommy, and loses his wife as the new season starts. “I spent a good part of my time in tears this entire season,” he told Variety.

In real life, Sam is a family man. Earlier this month, his wife Katharine Ross and their daughter Cleo Rose Elliott stepped out to support him at the Landman season two advance screening in Fort Worth, Texas.
So what do we know about his daughter, Cleo Rose Elliott?
She lives in Malibu

According to The Malibu Times, Cleo hails from Malibu and attended Malibu High and Colin McEwen High School. Speaking with Variety recently, Sam revealed that he still lives in Malibu with his wife and daughter. “I’m in the elements, and that’s the life that I chose, that Katharine and I and Cleo, all three of us, embrace,” he said.
Based on her Instagram posts, Cleo seems to be engaged to musician Jacob Rosenbaum, a bassist in the band Witch Rose.
What does she do for work?

Her Instagram bio states that she is an artist. The 41-year-old has formal training in classical Italian opera and learned to play instruments like the flute and guitar as a child, according to the Malibu Times. Her grandmother inspired her to pursue singing, and her debut album, No More Lies, was released in 2008.


According to the online outlet All That’s Interesting, Elliott reportedly attended the Joanne Baron/DW Brown Acting Studio in California and also has experience modelling. In 2020, she acted in the short film Murder for Dummies.
She once attacked her mother

In 2011, Katharine Ross reportedly filed a restraining order against her daughter. Per People, Elliott allegedly stabbed the Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid actress’ arm six times with scissors. Ross also claimed that Elliott “verbally and emotionally abused” her from a young age, developing violent tendencies as she entered her teenage years.
The pair seem to have a better relationship now though and often make red carpet appearances together.

data.title: South Koreans outraged after Canadian sports channel labels taekwondo ‘ninja training’
data.description:
South Koreans have slammed a major Canadian sports broadcaster for “ignorance” after it labelled a clip featuring high-flying taekwondo martial artists as “ninja training”.
Taekwondo is South Korea’s most famous martial art, while ninjas – the black-clad spies and assassins of Japan’s feudal era – are known for secrecy and stealth.
The controversy erupted after TSN, which bills itself as Canada’s sports leader, shared a clip of taekwondo practitioners performing acrobatic jump kick routines on social media last month with the caption “Ninja Training?”, prompting a wave of criticism from South Koreans demanding the description be corrected.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by 서경덕 (@seokyoungduk)



“Taekwondo is a Korean martial art. Why are you using a Japanese expression?” one user asked.
Some users described the mix-up as a “really upsetting incident” and questioned TSN’s “ignorance”, noting that taekwondo had been part of the Olympics since 2000. Others wondered if the phrasing reflected what they saw as Western enthusiasm for Japanese culture.
“If there’s something good, everything will be in Japan,” one commenter wrote.
Others suggested the broadcaster study more about Asian culture to avoid inflaming tensions between the neighbours who have been at loggerheads over Japan’s colonial past and territorial claims.
Seo Kyoung-duk, a professor at Sungshin Women’s University and a well-known advocate for correcting overseas misinformation about South Korea, said taekwondo has often been mislabelled abroad.
He noted that the International Olympic Committee had promoted taekwondo on social media under the hashtag judo – the Japanese martial art – during last year’s Paris Games. The organisation corrected the post after objections from Seo and others.
The academic said many foreigners did not have a proper understanding of taekwondo and that could have contributed to TSN’s mistake.
“Instead of criticism and anger, it’s important to push for immediate correction through proper complaints,” he said as quoted by The Korea Herald.


In 2021, Seo clashed with Japan over a museum in Tokyo that said Sohn Kee-chung, one of South Korea’s most famous athletes, was Japanese when he set a new Olympic marathon record at the 1936 Berlin Games. Korea was at the time part of the Japanese empire.
“It is a historical fact that Sohn participated in the Japanese team with the Japanese flag on his chest, but the world should be properly informed of the fact that he is Korean, not Japanese,” he told The Korea Times.
Based on the number of countries and studios registered with the taekwondo administering body in South Korea, an estimated 200 million people learn and practise the discipline.
In the 1970s, Seoul started to promote taekwondo internationally as the country’s main form of martial arts.
